questions.q: A pod is stuck in CrashLoopBackOff. What is the best first command to diagnose the issue?
questions.explanation: kubectl logs --previous retrieves the logs from the last terminated container instance. Since the container keeps crashing immediately, this is the best way to see what error occurred just before it exited.

questions.q: A pod's describe output shows 'Exit Code: 137'. What does this mean?
options: The container was killed due to an out-of-memory condition
The image could not be pulled
The container was evicted by the scheduler
questions.explanation: Exit code 137 = 128 + 9 (SIGKILL). This is the Linux signal sent when the OOM killer terminates a process for exceeding memory limits. The fix is to increase resources.limits.memory or fix the memory leak.

questions.q: Which command lets you run an interactive shell inside a running container?
questions.explanation: kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/sh opens an interactive shell inside the running container. Use /bin/bash if the image has bash. The -i flag keeps stdin open and -t allocates a TTY.

questions.q: A pod shows 'ImagePullBackOff'. Which section of kubectl describe pod output reveals the exact error?
questions.explanation: The Events section at the bottom of kubectl describe pod output shows the exact pull failure message, including the registry error, image name typo, or authentication failure details.

questions.q: kubectl debug can be used to attach a debug container to a pod even if the main container has no shell.
questions.explanation: True. kubectl debug -it <pod> --image=busybox --target=<container> creates an ephemeral debug container alongside the existing container, sharing the same process namespace. This is useful when the main image is distroless or scratch-based.
